data.title: Is Facebook down? Users report problems with Meta platforms
data.description:
TAMPA, Fla. (WFLA) — Is Facebook down? Users are reporting problems with Meta platforms.

Users took to non-Meta platforms like X on Friday to report that Facebook, Instagram and Threads appear to be displaying errors when they attempt to load or create new posts.

The error appears to affect users across the globe and affects both the app and website versions of the platforms, according to reports from DownDetector.

“We’re aware people are currently having trouble accessing our services. We’re working on it,” Andy Stone, a spokesperson for Meta, said in a post on X.

Developing

data.title: Why the changes to North Korea's constitution should scare us all
data.description:
Several recent changes to North Korea’s constitution, which the Supreme People’s Assembly adopted in March, have raised eyebrows and promoted considerable speculation and debate. The changes delete four articles from the previous version and amend 64 others.

Three amended articles in particular have attracted attention both in Seoul and internationally. The first change deletes all references to the reunification efforts of Kim Il Sung and Kim Jung Il — respectively Kim’s grandfather and father. It adds “national interest protection as an unwavering principle” to North Korea’s existing principles of “independence, peace and friendship.”

The new addition reflects Kim’s approach to foreign policy that increasingly balances the country’s longstanding dependence upon China with increasingly close ties to Russia.

The second change drops all previous references to “wartime pacification,” a staple of previous constitutions that were issued during the regimes of Kim’s father and grandfather and that envisaged reunification of the Korean peninsula based on common ethnicity. Instead, a 2024 amendment to the constitution termed South Korea a “hostile state” and dropped reunification as a North Korean objective.

The 2026 amended constitution reflects that view. It says nothing about unification and simply identifies the Republic of Korea as a separate state on its southern border and adds that North Korea “absolutely does not permit any encroachment upon its territory.”

The third change redefines Kim’s formal position as President of the State Affairs Commission from “Supreme Leader” to “Head of State.” For the first time, Kim is formally placed before the Supreme People’s Assembly in the order of state institutions. The amendment also transfers from the president of the assembly’s Standing Committee to Kim personally the authority to receive credentials and letters of recall from foreign ambassadors.  The change reflects the longstanding reality that only Kim, like his predecessors, represents the state.

The same amendment includes other changes that solidify Kim’s longstanding role as the country’s unchallenged leader. It specifies that only Kim, as president of the State Affairs Commission, can appoint or dismiss key officials, including the chairman of the Supreme People’s Assembly and its deputies, as well as the premier of the Cabinet. Previously these powers had been assigned to the Supreme People’s Assembly.

The amendment deletes the provision that would have the president answerable to the Supreme People’s Assembly and removes the Assembly’s authority to recall the president. It grants Kim the ability to veto the Assembly’s decisions. Most importantly, for the first time, it not only codifies Kim’s leadership of the armed forces, but also it explicitly stipulates Kim’s sole authority over the use of nuclear weapons without reference to any other government body.

Changes in constitutional language can mean very little. The 1936 Soviet constitution, formulated under Stalin’s leadership went further than any similar document in its extensive guarantees of civil rights that did not at all exist in practice. Even as the Kremlin released this document, Stalin was launching what became known as the Great Terror, which resulted in the death of millions.

On the other hand, the language in the constitution that codifies Kim’s power, and especially his taking sole control of North Korea’s expanding nuclear weapons program with no checks on his decision-making, is far from meaningless. Simply stated, it increases the risk of a nuclear crisis. As a senior South Korean analyst has put it, “nuclear weapons are no longer simply a deterrent against the United States or a bargaining chip in diplomatic negotiations. They are now an offensive instrument … with a lowered threshold for use.”

Moreover, North Korea has drawn upon its experience negotiating with the U.S. to advise Tehran how to manage its own talks with Washington. As a South Korean official indicated to me last week, North Korea is telling the Iranians, “No matter what, tell the USA that you will not give up nuclear weapons … the U.S. [negotiators] underestimate how much you can sacrifice the people’s life by having nuclear weapons. … Cheating the USA will be the first priority, easy to cheat.”

Not surprisingly, Seoul is evaluating the implications of the North’s constitutional amendments. Sung-lac Wi, South Korea’s national security advisor, informed me earlier this week that “we do take into account North Korea’s Constitution with notable revisions. It is my government’s position that we will continue to consistently pursue policies for peaceful co-existence on the Korean Peninsula.”

Whether the North also intends to pursue peaceful coexistence, having designated the South as a “hostile state,” and especially in light of its reported advice to Iran on negotiations with the U.S., is very much an open question. For that reason, and to ensure that Kim does not employ his nuclear arsenal pre-emptively, Washington’s commitment to South Korea’s security must remain ironclad.

Dov S. Zakheim is a senior adviser at the Center for Strategic and International Studies and vice chairman of the board for the Foreign Policy Research Institute. He was undersecretary of Defense (comptroller) and chief financial officer for the Department of Defense from 2001 to 2004 and a deputy undersecretary of Defense from 1985 to 1987.

data.title: Workers with disabilities deserve better than subminimum wages
data.description:
A bipartisan bill to end subminimum wages for workers with disabilities is sitting in Congress, with co-sponsors that range from Sen. Marsha Blackburn (R-Tenn.) to Sen. Kirsten Gillibrand (D-N.Y.). The argument against passing it has always been the same: that ending the practice would result in workers with disabilities losing their jobs. But new peer-reviewed evidence shows that argument is wrong. 

Fifteen states have already ended subminimum wages, and our study, just published in Labour Economics, examined what happened to workers in every one of them. Sheltered workshop employment fell by roughly 2,000 workers per state within two years, which is what the laws intended. But overall employment for workers with disabilities held steady. Employment rates, hours worked and competitive job placement all stayed at pre-policy levels in every state we examined, and the result held in the second and third years after each state’s policy change.  

The large-scale job losses opponents have predicted for decades did not appear in any state we studied. 

But the fiscal story is the more surprising finding. Welfare dependence among workers with disabilities dropped by 12.4 percent after states ended subminimum wages, and the effect grew in the second and third years rather than fading. Workers moved off public-benefit rolls and into the tax base. The fiscal direction of the policy runs opposite to what opponents have warned for decades.

We built these estimates from the first complete national dataset of every employer authorized to pay subminimum wages between 2015 and 2024, obtained through a public records request to the Labor Department, linked to 15 years of Current Population Survey data and analyzed with a staggered difference-in-differences design across all 15 states. 

The fiscal case strengthens further when paired with what we already know about vocational rehabilitation, the federal program that supports the transition from sheltered work to competitive employment.  

In our 2025 policy brief for the Department of Education’s National Clearinghouse of Rehabilitation Training Materials, my co-author and I documented that vocational rehabilitation services generate returns of $2.55 to $21.54 for every dollar invested, raise quarterly earnings by $1,442, and increase employment rates by 15.4 percentage points. The infrastructure to scale the transition already exists and already pays for itself. 

Virginia shows what a well-designed phase-out looks like in practice. As of April 2024, 129 workers remained under five active subminimum-wage certificates in the state, down from roughly 4,000 before 2020, on a path to the state’s 2030 deadline. A $13.8 million federal grant from the Department of Education funds the vocational rehabilitation counselors and the implementation project our team evaluates. 

The states that have already ended subminimum wages span the political spectrum, from Alaska and Tennessee to Maryland and California, with bipartisan governors and legislatures leading the change in nearly every case. National enrollment has fallen from roughly 220,000 workers in 2016 to about 40,000 in 2024 as states have steadily ended the practice on their own. A 2023 Government Accountability Office report found that nearly half of the remaining workers earn less than $3.50 an hour, and about one in 10 earns a dollar or less. 

The transition is not equally easy for every worker, and any honest case for reform has to say so. Workers with the most significant disabilities and the least competitive employment history face real barriers, and states that ended subminimum wages without investing in supported employment and job coaching left some workers without adequate transition assistance. The lesson is to design the federal phase-out correctly, with dedicated transition funding built in. 

The Transformation to Competitive Integrated Employment Act, which has been introduced in both the House and the Senate, does exactly that, pairing a six-year phase-out with dedicated federal transition funding. 

Fifteen states have shown the reform works. The peer-reviewed national evidence now confirms it. The bipartisan legislation is drafted, the federal infrastructure is in place, and the return on investment exceeds the cost by every estimate we have produced. Congress should pass the bill and finish the work the states have started.

Michelle Yin is an economist and professor at Northwestern University, where she founded and directs the RISEI Lab. Her work sits at the intersection of disability, education, labor markets and technology policy.

data.title: Schiff: Trump 'telling falsehood after falsehood' about war in Iran
data.description:
Sen. Adam Schiff (D-Calif.) said Thursday evening that President Trump’s account of the state of negotiations to end the Iran war “lacks a lot of credibility.” 

Trump earlier in the day threatened ramped up strikes on Tehran but later canceling them with a promise that Iran’s supreme leader had approved a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and engage in nuclear deal talks. 

“This is the problem when the president squanders his credibility by telling falsehood after falsehood throughout this presidency and his last, and that is the American people really don’t know when to believe him,” Schiff said during an appearance on CNNs “The Source.”

“And I don’t think there’s anything strategic about that ambiguity,” he told host Kaitlan Collins. “It doesn’t make us stronger that we can’t trust our own president.”

Esmail Baghaei, a spokesperson for Iran’s foreign ministry, said on Thursday that the U.S. and Tehran “had not reached a final conclusion” on a deal.

The president has signaled at least 38 times that a deal to end the hostilities that began on Feb. 28 was near, according to a CNN tally. 

“Every time the president talks about how eager Iran is for a deal, it’s basically a reveal that he’s talking about his own eagerness for a deal,” the California Democrat said Thursday. “And all I can say is I hope that he has more to show for it than an Iranian promise not to develop a bomb.”

Schiff also expressed his “fear” that the negotiations would end with a “face-saving announcement to agree at a later date by the president.” 

“That would be a terrible strategic loss for the country,” he added.

The senator noted that “only time will tell” on the negotiation effort between the U.S. and Iran. 

“But one thing is perfectly clear,” he continued. “A tremendous amount of damage has been done, and American families are paying the price.”

Energy costs have soared amid the conflict, which has effectively halted the flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz. Around a fifth of the world’s oil consumption travels through this waterway on a daily basis under normal conditions. 

The average cost of standard gas in the U.S. was up to over $4 a gallon on Friday morning, a 31 percent increase from the average a year ago, according to AAA. In Schiff’s home state of California, average gas prices have jumped from around $4.66 a year ago to $5.78 on Friday — a 24 percent increase.

data.title: Judge demands Trump administration comply with asylum order
data.description:
A federal judge chastised the Trump administration for ignoring a ruling last week ordering them to immediately resume processing of immigration applications paused in the wake of a deadly shooting on National Guard officers.

U.S. District Judge John McConnell last week found the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) was unlawfully pausing immigration processing for nationals from a number of countries, halting legal immigration and leaving migrants in limbo as they seek green cards or await naturalization ceremonies.

Last Friday McConnell vacated the policies, but plaintiffs went back to the judge, saying DHS had failed to begin processing anew.

“It should almost go without saying—but the Court will say it anyway for the sake of “clarify[ing]” the Government’s ‘current obligations,’…that court orders vacating and setting aside agency policies have immediate effect once they are issued,” the Rhode-Island based McConnell wrote in his ruling.

“There is no excuse this time; the Government has an obligation to immediately comply with this Order.”

The reprimand is the latest instance of DHS or its component agencies violating court orders.

A federal judge in Minnesota earlier this year noted that U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) had failed to comply with 96 court orders from 74 different immigration cases.

data.title: Did Tyler Robinson prosecutors violate gag order?
data.description:
(NewsNation) — A Utah judge will hold an evidentiary hearing Friday over whether prosecutors violated a gag order by making comments to the media about the criminal case against Tyler Robinson, who is accused of killing conservative political activist Charlie Kirk. 

Lawyers for Robinson requested State District Judge Tony Graf to look into comments made by Deputy County Attorney Chris Ballard to outlets, including TMZ and Fox News. 

Robinson’s defense attorneys called Ballard’s actions a contempt of court. 

They took issue with public statements the prosecutors made, including an interview on “Fox & Friends,” and said the lawyers must hand over all internal communications regarding media outreach.

The judge found that defense made a sufficient preliminary showing the prosecutors might have violated the pretrial publicity rule and ordered an evidentiary hearing to determine whether any violations were knowing and intentional. 

Prosecutors have said a hearing and additional internal communications are unnecessary to determine whether Ballard’s comments violated the gag order, adding that the judge should evaluate the statements themselves to decide whether they crossed the line.

They also said they had a duty to publicly respond to misleading media coverage to prevent tainting the jury pool and to correct what they viewed as inaccurate characterizations of evidence.

Will the public have access to Tyler Robinson’s trial?

Robinson, 23, is accused of fatally shooting Kirk on the Utah Valley University campus last September and faces charges, including aggravated murder. 

Graf had previously ruled that media, and the public will not be barred from parts of a key upcoming hearing. 

The judge denied Robinson’s previous request to close all or part of the preliminary hearing, saying his attorneys failed to show a public hearing would create a “realistic likelihood” of prejudicing his right to a fair trial.

“The public and the media enjoy a presumptive right to access court proceedings, including preliminary hearings,” he said.

Graf added there are other methods to protect a defendant’s right to a fair trial, including increasing the jury pool, utilizing detailed juror questionnaires and conducting thorough jury selection.

His ruling centers on a hearing scheduled for July 6-10, where prosecutors will lay out their evidence and a judge will decide whether the case should go to trial. It will be the most significant presentation of evidence in the case so far.

data.title: Trump has better options for intelligence and attorney general nominations
data.description:
On Monday, President Trump formally nominated Todd Blanche as attorney general. Blanche has been acting attorney general since Pam Bondi was removed from office on April 2.

The question Trump must face is whether Blanche can be confirmed. He has clearly auditioned for the job on a full-time basis. Trump wants an attorney general who will bend the Justice Department to his wishes, which are dominated by his retribution campaign against perceived political opponents. 

Blanche issued a well-deserved indictment of the Southern Poverty Law Center, which over the years has become a left-wing lawfare operation. On the other side of the ledger, though, Blanche signed off on a settlement of Trump’s lawsuit against the government he now runs, which provides immunity for Trump and family, and was to create a fund totaling nearly $1.8 billion in taxpayer dollars to be distributed to those who claim legal mistreatment at the hands of the federal government.

According to Blanche’s testimony before the House Appropriations Committee on June 2, the administration will no longer pursue the fund. But Blanche maintained that the immunity from tax prosecution for Trump and his family remains in force. That is the equivalent of a get-out-of-jail-free card before anyone has determined you did anything wrong. It is a free pass for Trump to do whatever he wants to benefit himself at the expense of America’s taxpayers.

That blanket immunity for the Trump family will be a huge problem for Blanche in his confirmation hearing. The attorney general is not the president’s lawyer, even if Blanche was Trump’s personal lawyer in three criminal cases in the past. The attorney general is supposed to represent the interests of the United States.

Can Blanche be confirmed? Maybe. It will be close. Trump’s recent political actions in aiding the primary election defeat of Sens. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) and John Cornyn (R-Texas) complicates Blanche’s outlook. And even then, even a successful confirmation could turn into a pyrrhic victory for Trump. 

On another nomination, the president is on record saying that the Bill Pulte appointment as acting director of National Intelligence is temporary. Yesterday, Trump made good on that by nominating Jay Clayton as director of National Intelligence. Clayton is currently the U. S. Attorney for the Southern District of New York and served as chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission in Trump’s first term. He received 61 confirmation votes for that position  including nine from Democrats. With no major negatives as of now Clayton would appear to be on a path to confirmation.

Trump has clearly capitulated to Senate Republicans who were angry at the White House for trying to install Pulte as director of National Intelligence. Pulte has zero experience in national intelligence and had zero chance of ever being confirmed. Looking at current polling and Trump’s dismal approval rating, he cannot afford to lose on such a high profile issue involving either Pulte or Blanche.

EPA administrator Lee Zeldin would make a solid attorney general. The former New York congressman. who ran a surprisingly strong race in 2022 for governor of the very blue New York State, served on the Foreign Affairs Committee and the subcommittee that deals with terrorism issues.

Most importantly, Zeldin has nothing to do with the all-encompassing grant of immunity to the Trump family on future tax liability — a grant that feeds the belief that America works for the rich and powerful but not for the rest of us.

As for the director of National Intelligence position, Trump probably should have turned to Rep. Elise Stefanik (R-N.Y.), whom he once nominated as ambassador to the United Nations. Subsequently, concerned about maintaining the slim Republican House majority and the unspoken but very realistic possibility of losing a special election in Stefanik’s Canadian-border district due to Trump’s insulting rhetoric toward Canada, he withdrew her nomination.

After Stefanik announced her uphill campaign for New York governor, Trump refused to endorse her, proving once again that in Trump world, loyalty is a one-way street. Thereafter, Stefanik announced she would not seek any office in 2026, and Trump quickly endorsed Nassau County Executive Bruce Blakeman for New York governor.

So Trump owes Stefanik. Given her congressional experience on the Armed Services Committee and the Select Committee on Intelligence, she would be a solid choice for director of National Intelligence, and she would likely be confirmed.

Trump is in a position where he needs an easy win. Stefanik for director of National Intelligence and Zeldin as the nominee for attorney general would have given the president two solid nominees without the self inflicted political headaches.

In politics, as in sports, missed opportunities will cost you.

Kevin Igoe is the former deputy chief of staff of the Republican National Committee and former executive director of the Maryland Republican Party. He served as chief of staff of the Maryland Department of Budget and Management and was a Reagan White House appointee.

data.title: Rubio compares UFC to moon landing
data.description:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio on Thursday compared UFC to America’s monumental 1969 moon landing ahead of the UFC Freedom 250 event at the White House this Sunday. 

“When President Kennedy announced that we were going to put a man on the moon and return him safely to the earth, no one thought that was possible, and we did it,” Rubio said in comments to reporters during a signing ceremony at the White House. 

“We are a nation founded on doing what no one else dared to do, and no one else aspired to do,” he continued. “And at some level, that’s what this whole company, what UFC has been.” 

Rubio and Dana White, president and CEO of UFC, signed a memorandum of understanding on Thursday to form a public-private partnership ahead of the cage fights this weekend at the White House. The secretary referred to the mixed martial arts company as the “United Nations of fighting” during the ceremony.

Trump, a fan of the sport, has a close relationship with White.

The president directed the installation of an arena on the White House’s South Lawn to host UFC fights as part of a series of celebrations to mark America’s 250th birthday. The event will be held on his birthday this Sunday. 

The construction of the 4,500-seat arena began at the end of May. Additionally, “massive screens” on The Ellipse will allow as many as 100,000 people to watch the fight, the president told reporters in April.

The president has compared the structure to the Eiffel Tower and suggested that the ring could be a permanent addition to the White House. 

“Maybe we’ll just host weekly fights between people in politics, you know, and settle our scores that way,” Rubio joked on Thursday.

data.title: Alfredo sauce sold in 41 states recalled over salmonella concerns
data.description:
(NewsNation) — The Coffee Connexion Co. is voluntarily recalling 913 cases of Alfredo sauce sold in 41 states over concerns about salmonella contamination.

The Tennessee-based company initiated the recall on May 6 because its sauce contains dry milk powder that its supplier previously recalled due to potential salmonella contamination, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) said in an enforcement report.

The sauce was distributed to the following states, according to the FDA:

Alabama, Arkansas, Arizona, California, Colorado, Florida, Georgia, Illinois, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Massachusetts, Maryland, Maine, Michigan, Minnesota, Missouri, Mississippi, Montana, North Carolina, Nebraska, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, Ohio, Oklahoma, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, South Carolina, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, Virginia, Washington, Wisconsin and Wyoming.

The FDA designated the recall a Class I, the agency’s most serious classification, on June 4, meaning there’s a “reasonable probability” that consuming the product “will cause adverse health consequences or death.”

Salmonella bacteria can cause salmonellosis, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). Symptoms, which include diarrhea, fever, and stomach cramps, can set in six hours to six days after exposure.

While most people can recover on their own within four days to a week, infections can be more serious for young children, the elderly, and those with weakened immune systems. Some may even require medical treatment or hospitalization, the CDC says.

data.title: Paxton warns Big 12 of potential legal action over any Texas Tech Sorsby sanctions
data.description:
Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton (R), a candidate for governor, is warning schools across the Big 12 Conference that the state would consider suing any institution that sanctions Texas Tech over a decision to play a quarterback who sued the NCAA to regain his eligibility.

In a letter to Big 12 Commissioner Brett Yormark and Kansas Chancellor Douglas Girod, Paxton wrote any sanctioning of Texas Tech “would be a per se violation of federal and state antitrust laws — a naked horizontal agreement among competitors to disadvantage Texas Tech by cutting off access to the resources it needs to compete,” according to The Associated Press.

It comes amid reports some Big 12 schools are considering the possibility of refusing to play Texas Tech should quarterback Brendan Sorsby compete.

Such a move would constitute a “breach of contract and tortious interference” the Texas government argued in its letter.

Sorsby, one of the top players in the transfer portal last season, was suspended by the NCAA for violating its policies related to gambling.

The quarterback has admitted to betting thousands of dollars on multiple sports, including college football games involving teams he was a member of early in his career.

Sorsby recently completed a rehabilitation program from gambling addiction and sued the NCAA asking a judge to reinstate him, with his legal team arguing the ability to play football is essential to his recovery process.

A judge earlier this week ruled in favor of Sorsby, who is slated to miss the first two games of the season as a condition of his reinstatement as laid out by the court.

The NCAA blasted the ruling and has pointed to Sorsby’s case as it urges Congress to pass legislation granting it antitrust exemptions it says would allow it to better enforce its rules on issues relating to eligibility, name image and likeness and revenue sharing.
